chunk_summary: Guidance on steroid tapering and biologic dosing in low-weight pediatric IBD patients, emphasizing growth preservation, infection risk, and nutrition integration.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of systemic corticosteroids in pediatric Crohn’s disease induction and tapering strategies.
Learn weight-based biologic dosing adjustments for low-weight children with IBD.
Recognize the importance of integrating nutrition and pharmacotherapy in pediatric IBD management.
Assess infection risks, including tuberculosis, before anti-TNF escalation.
key_facts: Systemic corticosteroids should not be used as long-term therapy due to risks like growth suppression and bone health deterioration.
Infliximab dosing: 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, and 6, then every 8 weeks; higher doses (up to 10 mg/kg) or shorter intervals may be needed for children <30 kg or with low albumin.
Adalimumab dosing: >40 kg: 160 mg week 0, 80 mg week 2, then 40 mg every other week; <40 kg: 80 mg week 0, 40 mg week 2, then 20 mg maintenance; escalation to weekly injections in selected cases.
Vedolizumab and ustekinumab are alternatives when anti-TNF therapy fails, requiring infection-risk monitoring.
Nutrition plans should address protein-energy adequacy, anemia correction, and vitamin D/calcium deficits alongside pharmacotherapy.
reference_quote: Plan the taper and the maintenance strategy on day one (immunomodulator and/or biologic pathway) to avoid prolonged steroid exposure that suppresses growth, worsens bone health, and increases infection risk.
document_key_topics: Steroid tapering in pediatric Crohn’s disease
Biologic dosing adjustments for low-weight children
Nutrition and pharmacotherapy integration in IBD
Infection risk assessment in anti-TNF therapy
